title: Conditions & Loops
description: Teach your code to make decisions and repeat tasks — the engine behind every forensic scanner and log parser
learning_outcomes: Use if, elif, and else to make decisions based on evidence data
Apply comparison operators (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=) to evaluate conditions
Combine conditions with logical operators (and, or, not) for complex rules
Write for loops to iterate through evidence collections, log entries, and byte arrays
Use while loops for continuous monitoring and conditional scanning
Control loop flow with break (stop scanning), continue (skip item), and pass
Nest conditions inside loops for sophisticated evidence filtering
Use range() to generate sequences for port scanning and brute-force patterns
Build enumerate() loops for tracking position in evidence lists
Implement real forensic patterns: threat detection, log filtering, password validation, file scanning
Understand loop performance and when to use break for efficiency
Write match/case statements (Python 3.10+) for structured pattern matching

explanation_styles.analogy: Think of if/elif/else like a security checkpoint with multiple lanes. The guard checks your ID (the condition). If you're VIP (True), you go to the fast lane. Elif you're staff, you go to the staff entrance. Else (everyone else), you join the regular queue. Only ONE lane gets used — once a condition is True, the rest are skipped.
explanation_styles.visual: Picture a flowchart: Diamond shapes are decisions (if), rectangles are actions (code blocks). Each diamond has two arrows: YES (True) goes one way, NO (False) goes the other. Following the arrows = running your code.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Python evaluates the if condition. Step 2: If True, run that block and SKIP all elif/else. Step 3: If False, check the next elif. Step 4: If all elif are False, run the else block. Step 5: Continue after the whole if/elif/else structure.
explanation_styles.code_trace: For: if x > 10: — Python looks up x's value (say 7), compares 7 > 10 (False), skips the if block, checks elif, and so on. Trace each comparison like reading a decision tree.
explanation_styles.comparison: Java: if (x > 10) { ... } else if (x > 5) { ... } else { ... }. Python: if x > 10: ... elif x > 5: ... else: ... — No parentheses needed, no curly braces, just clean indentation. Easier to read, same logic!

lessons.title: Conditions & Loops — The Decision Engine of Forensic Tools

theory.introduction:
Every forensic tool you'll ever use makes DECISIONS and REPEATS tasks. When a firewall checks a packet, it asks: 'Is this IP address blocked? Is this port allowed? Does this pattern match a known attack?' — those are CONDITIONS (if/elif/else). When an antivirus scans your hard drive, it checks file after file after file — that's a LOOP (for/while).

Think of it this way: in Topic 1 you learned to SPEAK (print). In Topic 2 you learned to REMEMBER (variables). Now in Topic 3 you learn to THINK (conditions) and SEARCH (loops). Together, these make your code intelligent.

Imagine you're a forensic analyst with 10,000 log entries. Without conditions, you can't filter suspicious ones. Without loops, you'd have to check each entry manually. With both? You write 10 lines of code that automatically scans all 10,000 entries, flags the suspicious ones, counts the threats, and generates a report. That's the power you're about to unlock.

This topic is often considered the HARDEST for beginners — but also the most rewarding. Once conditions and loops click, you can build REAL tools: port scanners, log analysers, password validators, threat detectors, and file scanners. Let's make your code think!

VM TIP: On your Kali Linux VM, try: nmap -sT 127.0.0.1 — that's a port scanner using loops to check each port and conditions to report which are open. By the end of this topic, you'll understand the LOGIC behind it.
theory.why_important: Conditions and loops are the engine of EVERY cybersecurity tool in existence. Firewall rules are if/elif/else chains. Port scanners are for loops over port ranges. Brute-force tools are while loops with break on success. Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) are while loops running conditions against every network packet 24/7. Log parsers combine for loops (iterate entries) with if conditions (filter suspicious ones). Without these, your code can only run in a straight line — with them, it can make intelligent decisions and process unlimited amounts of data automatically. This is where you go from 'student' to 'tool builder'.

key_concepts.explanation:
The if statement checks a condition — if it's True, the indented code block runs. If False, it's skipped. Think of it like a security guard at a door: if you have the right badge, you get in. Otherwise, nothing happens and you walk past.

The condition can be any expression that evaluates to True or False: comparisons (x > 10), equality checks (password == 'secret'), membership tests ('admin' in user_roles), or even just a variable (if file_exists:). Remember from Topic 2: any truthy value counts as True, any falsy value counts as False.
key_concepts.example:
# if statement — the basic decision
port = 443

if port == 443:
    print("HTTPS detected — encrypted connection")

if port < 1024:
    print("Well-known port — likely a standard service")

# Using truthiness
suspicious_files = ["malware.exe", "keylogger.py"]
if suspicious_files:  # Non-empty list = True
    print(f"WARNING: {len(suspicious_files)} suspicious files found!")
key_concepts.ai_hint: if checks ONE condition. If True → run the block. If False → skip it entirely. The colon : and indentation are REQUIRED!

key_concepts.explanation:
if/else gives you exactly two paths: if the condition is True, run block A. Otherwise (else), run block B. Think of a traffic light: if green, GO. Else (red), STOP. There's no in-between — one path or the other.

In forensics, if/else is used everywhere: if hash matches → evidence verified, else → evidence may be tampered with. If file is encrypted → flag for decryption, else → proceed with analysis. If login attempt count exceeds threshold → block the account, else → allow access.
key_concepts.example:
# if/else — two paths
login_attempts = 5
max_attempts = 3

if login_attempts > max_attempts:
    print("ALERT: Account locked — too many failed attempts!")
    print(f"Attempts: {login_attempts}/{max_attempts}")
else:
    print("Login attempts within normal range")
    print(f"Attempts: {login_attempts}/{max_attempts}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: else has NO condition — it catches EVERYTHING that didn't match the if. Think of it as the 'default' or 'otherwise' path.

key_concepts.explanation:
elif (else if) lets you check multiple conditions in sequence. Python checks each one from top to bottom and runs the FIRST one that's True. Think of it like a security classification system: check if it's CRITICAL first, then HIGH, then MEDIUM, then LOW. Once a match is found, the rest are skipped.

elif is CRUCIAL in forensics for: threat severity classification, port identification, file type detection, response code analysis, and risk scoring. You can have as many elif blocks as needed — there's no limit.
key_concepts.example:
# elif chain — threat severity classification
severity_score = 8.5

if severity_score >= 9.0:
    level = "CRITICAL"
    colour = "RED"
elif severity_score >= 7.0:
    level = "HIGH"
    colour = "ORANGE"
elif severity_score >= 4.0:
    level = "MEDIUM"
    colour = "YELLOW"
elif severity_score >= 1.0:
    level = "LOW"
    colour = "GREEN"
else:
    level = "INFO"
    colour = "BLUE"

print(f"Score: {severity_score} → {level} ({colour})")
key_concepts.ai_hint: elif checks are ordered — Python stops at the FIRST True condition. Put the most specific/critical checks FIRST, general ones LAST.

key_concepts.explanation:
Comparison operators compare two values and return True or False. They're the building blocks of every condition.

== (equal to) — does this value MATCH that value? Used for hash comparison, password checking.
!= (not equal) — are these DIFFERENT? Used to find anomalies, mismatches.
> and < (greater/less than) — is this MORE or LESS? Used for thresholds, file sizes.
>= and <= (greater/less or equal) — includes the boundary value. Used for ranges.

WARNING: == is comparison, = is assignment! Writing if x = 5: is a common bug (Python will actually give a SyntaxError for this, which is helpful). Always use == for comparison.
key_concepts.example:
# Comparison operators in forensic contexts
file_size = 1048576  # 1 MB
hash1 = "abc123"
hash2 = "abc123"
port = 22

# Equality
print(f"Hashes match: {hash1 == hash2}")
print(f"Hashes differ: {hash1 != hash2}")

# Size comparisons
print(f"Over 1 MB: {file_size > 1000000}")
print(f"Under 5 MB: {file_size < 5000000}")

# Range check
print(f"Well-known port (0-1023): {0 <= port <= 1023}")

# String comparison (alphabetical)
print(f"'apple' < 'banana': {'apple' < 'banana'}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: == checks equality (are they the same?). = assigns a value (put this in that variable). Mixing them up is one of the most common bugs in programming!

key_concepts.concept: Logical Operators — Combining Conditions (and, or, not)
key_concepts.explanation:
Logical operators combine multiple conditions into one: 'and' requires BOTH to be True, 'or' requires AT LEAST ONE to be True, 'not' flips True to False and vice versa.

Think of 'and' like a security system that needs BOTH a keycard AND a PIN code. Think of 'or' like a building with two entrances — either door works. Think of 'not' like a bouncer who lets everyone in EXCEPT people on the blacklist.

In forensic rules: 'if port == 22 and country != "US"' flags SSH from foreign IPs. 'if file_size > 1000000 or is_encrypted' flags large or encrypted files. 'if not is_analysed' finds evidence that hasn't been processed yet.
key_concepts.example:
# Logical operators — building complex forensic rules
port = 22
country = "RU"
is_encrypted = True
file_size = 5000000

# AND — both must be true
if port == 22 and country != "US":
    print("ALERT: Foreign SSH connection!")

# OR — at least one must be true
if file_size > 1000000 or is_encrypted:
    print("FLAG: Large or encrypted file detected")

# NOT — inverts the condition
is_analysed = False
if not is_analysed:
    print("PENDING: Evidence awaiting analysis")

# Combining all three
if (port < 1024 and country == "RU") or is_encrypted:
    print("HIGH RISK: Suspicious connection profile")
key_concepts.ai_hint: and = BOTH must be True. or = AT LEAST ONE must be True. not = flips the result. Use parentheses to make complex conditions clear!

key_concepts.explanation:
The for loop iterates through a collection — one item at a time. Think of it like a forensic analyst going through a box of evidence bags: pick up the first bag, examine it, move to the next, repeat until the box is empty.

You can loop through: lists (evidence items), strings (characters), ranges of numbers (port ranges), dictionaries (key-value pairs), and files (lines of a log). The for loop knows exactly how many items there are and stops automatically when it reaches the end.

In forensics, for loops are used to scan file lists, parse log entries, check port ranges, iterate through hash databases, process evidence collections, and generate reports.
key_concepts.example:
# for loop — scanning evidence

# Loop through a list
evidence = ["laptop.img", "phone.tar", "usb.zip", "email.pst"]
for item in evidence:
    print(f"Scanning: {item}")

# Loop through a string (character by character)
hex_str = "4D5A"
print("\nHex bytes:")
for char in hex_str:
    print(f"  Character: {char} (ASCII: {ord(char)})")

# Loop with index using enumerate()
print("\nEvidence inventory:")
for i, item in enumerate(evidence, 1):
    print(f"  [{i}] {item}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: for item in collection: gives you one item at a time. enumerate() adds a counter. Both are used in EVERY forensic scanning tool!

key_concepts.explanation:
range() generates a sequence of numbers. range(5) gives 0,1,2,3,4. range(1,6) gives 1,2,3,4,5. range(0, 256) gives all possible byte values. range(1, 1025) gives all well-known ports.

Think of range() like setting the dial on a scanning tool — you specify the start, stop, and step. It's essential for port scanning (check ports 1-1024), brute-force logic (try combinations 0000-9999), and byte analysis (examine each byte value 0-255).

range(start, stop, step): start is inclusive, stop is EXCLUSIVE (very important!), step is the increment. range(0, 100, 10) gives 0, 10, 20, 30... 90.
key_concepts.example:
# range() — generating sequences for scanning

# Basic range
print("Ports to scan:")
for port in range(80, 86):
    print(f"  Checking port {port}...")

# range with step
print("\nEven ports:")
for port in range(80, 90, 2):
    print(f"  Port {port}")

# Counting down
print("\nCountdown:")
for i in range(5, 0, -1):
    print(f"  {i}...")
print("  SCAN COMPLETE!")

# All byte values (0-255)
print(f"\nByte range: {list(range(0, 256))[:5]}...{list(range(0, 256))[-3:]}")
print(f"Total byte values: {len(range(0, 256))}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: range(start, stop) — stop is EXCLUSIVE! range(1, 5) gives 1,2,3,4 (not 5). This trips up everyone at first!

key_concepts.explanation:
A while loop keeps running AS LONG AS its condition is True. Think of it like a security camera that records continuously WHILE the building is open. Or an IDS (Intrusion Detection System) that monitors network traffic WHILE the server is running.

while loops are perfect when you DON'T KNOW how many iterations you need: keep trying passwords until one works, keep reading log lines until end of file, keep monitoring until a threat is detected, keep retrying a connection until it succeeds or times out.

DANGER: If the condition never becomes False, you get an INFINITE LOOP! Always make sure something inside the loop eventually makes the condition False, or use break to exit.
key_concepts.example:
# while loop — continuous monitoring

# Brute-force PIN simulation
import random
target_pin = "1234"
attempts = 0
max_attempts = 10

while attempts < max_attempts:
    attempts += 1
    guess = f"{random.randint(0, 9999):04d}"
    
    if guess == target_pin:
        print(f"PIN CRACKED: {guess} (attempt {attempts})")
        break
else:
    # This runs if the while loop finishes WITHOUT break
    print(f"Failed after {max_attempts} attempts")

print(f"Total attempts: {attempts}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: while = 'keep going UNTIL this condition becomes False'. for = 'do this FOR each item in a collection'. Use while when you don't know how many iterations you need!

key_concepts.explanation:
break STOPS the loop immediately — like a detective saying 'Found the evidence, stop searching!' continue SKIPS the current item and moves to the next — like 'This one's clean, next!' pass does NOTHING — it's a placeholder for code you haven't written yet.

In forensics: break when you find the evidence you're looking for (first match). continue to skip files that don't match your criteria (wrong extension, too small). pass as a placeholder in your scan function while you work on the detection logic.

break makes loops more EFFICIENT — why scan 10,000 files if you found the threat at file #47?
key_concepts.ai_hint: break = 'STOP the entire loop'. continue = 'skip THIS iteration, go to next'. pass = 'do nothing (placeholder)'. break is your efficiency tool!

key_concepts.explanation:
You can put loops inside loops and conditions inside loops (or loops inside conditions!). This is called NESTING. Think of it like a building search: the OUTER loop goes floor by floor, the INNER loop checks each room on that floor, and CONDITIONS inside decide if evidence is found.

In forensics, nesting is used for: scanning multiple hosts with multiple ports (outer = hosts, inner = ports), checking multiple log files for multiple threat signatures, comparing multiple hash values against multiple databases, and generating combination lists for testing.

Keep nesting shallow (2-3 levels max) for readability. Deep nesting makes code hard to understand.
key_concepts.example:
# Nested loops — multi-dimensional scanning

# Scan multiple hosts on multiple ports
hosts = ["192.168.1.1", "192.168.1.2"]
ports = [22, 80, 443]

print("=== NETWORK SCAN ===")
for host in hosts:
    print(f"\nHost: {host}")
    for port in ports:
        # Simulate: some ports are open
        is_open = (host.endswith(".1") and port == 80) or port == 443
        status = "OPEN" if is_open else "closed"
        marker = ">>>" if is_open else "   "
        print(f"  {marker} Port {port}: {status}")

print("\nScan complete!")
key_concepts.ai_hint: Nesting = loops inside loops. The inner loop runs COMPLETELY for each iteration of the outer loop. 2 hosts × 3 ports = 6 total checks!

key_concepts.explanation:
The 'in' operator checks if a value exists INSIDE a collection. 'admin' in users returns True if 'admin' is in the users list. 'exe' in filename checks if 'exe' appears anywhere in the filename string.

Think of 'in' like checking a watchlist — is this IP on our blocklist? Is this file extension in our suspicious list? Is this keyword in the log entry? It's one of the most-used operators in forensic scripts because evidence analysis is fundamentally about SEARCHING.

'not in' is the inverse — 'if ip not in whitelist:' flags IPs that AREN'T approved.
key_concepts.example:
# in operator — searching evidence

# Check against a blocklist
blocklist = ["10.0.0.1", "172.16.0.99", "192.168.1.666"]
test_ip = "172.16.0.99"

if test_ip in blocklist:
    print(f"BLOCKED: {test_ip} is on the blocklist!")

# Check file extensions
suspicious_ext = [".exe", ".bat", ".cmd", ".ps1", ".vbs"]
filename = "report.exe"

for ext in suspicious_ext:
    if filename.endswith(ext):
        print(f"WARNING: {filename} has suspicious extension {ext}")
        break

# Search within strings
log_line = "2025-02-28 FAILED login from 10.0.0.5"
if "FAILED" in log_line:
    print(f"Alert: {log_line}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: 'in' searches INSIDE a collection or string. Use it like a forensic search tool: 'Is this item in my evidence database?'

key_concepts.explanation:
The ternary operator (conditional expression) lets you write a simple if/else in ONE line: result = value_if_true if condition else value_if_false. Think of it like a quick YES/NO stamp on an evidence form — no need for a full page when a single line will do.

Use it for simple assignments where a full if/else block would be overkill. Don't nest ternary operators — that makes code unreadable. If the logic is complex, use a regular if/else.
key_concepts.example:
# Ternary operator — quick decisions

port = 443
service = "HTTPS" if port == 443 else "Unknown"
print(f"Port {port}: {service}")

# Status flags
files_found = 0
status = "Evidence found" if files_found > 0 else "No evidence"
print(f"Status: {status}")

# Risk level
score = 7.5
risk = "HIGH" if score >= 7 else "MEDIUM" if score >= 4 else "LOW"
print(f"Score {score}: {risk}")

# Formatting
for port in [22, 80, 443, 8080, 3306]:
    known = "well-known" if port < 1024 else "registered"
    print(f"  Port {port:>5}: {known}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: Ternary = one-line if/else. Great for simple assignments. For complex logic, stick to regular if/elif/else — readability matters more than brevity!

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Security Alert System
complete_examples.description: Basic if/elif/else chain — classify threats by severity
complete_examples.code:
# Security alert classification system

alerts = [
    {"type": "Login", "attempts": 15, "source": "external"},
    {"type": "Login", "attempts": 2, "source": "internal"},
    {"type": "File Access", "attempts": 1, "source": "internal"},
    {"type": "Port Scan", "attempts": 500, "source": "external"},
    {"type": "Login", "attempts": 8, "source": "external"},
]

print("SECURITY ALERT DASHBOARD")
print("=" * 55)
print(f"{'Type':<15}{'Attempts':>8}{'Source':>12}{'Severity':>12}{'Action':>10}")
print("-" * 55)

for alert in alerts:
    at = alert["type"]
    attempts = alert["attempts"]
    source = alert["source"]
    
    # Classify severity
    if attempts >= 100 and source == "external":
        severity = "CRITICAL"
        action = "BLOCK"
    elif attempts >= 10 and source == "external":
        severity = "HIGH"
        action = "ALERT"
    elif attempts >= 5 or source == "external":
        severity = "MEDIUM"
        action = "LOG"
    else:
        severity = "LOW"
        action = "MONITOR"
    
    print(f"{at:<15}{attempts:>8}{source:>12}{severity:>12}{action:>10}")

print("=" * 55)
complete_examples.output:
SECURITY ALERT DASHBOARD
=======================================================
Type           Attempts      Source    Severity    Action
-------------------------------------------------------
Login                15    external        HIGH     ALERT
Login                 2    internal         LOW   MONITOR
File Access           1    internal         LOW   MONITOR
Port Scan           500    external    CRITICAL     BLOCK
Login                 8    external      MEDIUM       LOG
=======================================================
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This demonstrates a real-world security pattern:
1. A list of alert dictionaries — each with type, attempts, and source.
2. A for loop iterates through every alert.
3. if/elif/else classifies each alert based on MULTIPLE criteria using 'and' and 'or'.
4. The most severe check comes FIRST (>=100 AND external), then less severe.
5. Each alert gets a severity level and recommended action.

This is EXACTLY how Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems like Splunk and QRadar work — they apply rules to classify incoming alerts. You just built a mini SIEM!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a counter for each severity level and print a summary at the bottom: 'Critical: 1, High: 1, Medium: 1, Low: 2'.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Port Scanner Logic
complete_examples.description: Use for loop with range() to scan ports and identify services
complete_examples.code:
# Mini port scanner — the logic behind Nmap!
# (This is the LOGIC only — real scanning happens on Kali VM)

# Known services database
KNOWN_PORTS = {
    21: "FTP", 22: "SSH", 23: "Telnet", 25: "SMTP",
    53: "DNS", 80: "HTTP", 110: "POP3", 143: "IMAP",
    443: "HTTPS", 993: "IMAPS", 3306: "MySQL",
    3389: "RDP", 5432: "PostgreSQL", 8080: "HTTP-Alt"
}

# Simulate which ports are "open"
open_ports = {22, 80, 443, 3306, 8080}

print("PORT SCAN RESULTS")
print(f"Target: 192.168.1.100")
print(f"Scanning ports 1-1024 + common high ports...")
print("-" * 50)
print(f"{'PORT':>6} | {'STATE':<8} | {'SERVICE'}")
print("-" * 50)

open_count = 0
for port in sorted(KNOWN_PORTS.keys()):
    if port in open_ports:
        state = "OPEN"
        open_count += 1
        service = KNOWN_PORTS[port]
        print(f"{port:>6} | {state:<8} | {service}")

print("-" * 50)
print(f"\n{open_count} open ports found out of {len(KNOWN_PORTS)} checked")

# Risk assessment
risky_ports = {21, 23, 3389}  # FTP, Telnet, RDP are risky
found_risky = risky_ports & open_ports  # Set intersection
if found_risky:
    print(f"\nWARNING: Risky open ports: {found_risky}")
    for p in found_risky:
        print(f"  Port {p} ({KNOWN_PORTS[p]}) should be closed!")
else:
    print("\nNo high-risk ports detected.")
complete_examples.output:
PORT SCAN RESULTS
Target: 192.168.1.100
Scanning ports 1-1024 + common high ports...
--------------------------------------------------
  PORT | STATE    | SERVICE
--------------------------------------------------
    22 | OPEN     | SSH
    80 | OPEN     | HTTP
   443 | OPEN     | HTTPS
  3306 | OPEN     | MySQL
  8080 | OPEN     | HTTP-Alt
--------------------------------------------------

5 open ports found out of 14 checked

WARNING: Risky open ports: {22}
  Port 22 (SSH) should be closed!
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This simulates the LOGIC of a real port scanner:
1. KNOWN_PORTS dictionary maps port numbers to service names.
2. open_ports set simulates which ports responded (in real tools, this would be a TCP connection test).
3. The for loop checks each known port — 'if port in open_ports' tests membership.
4. Set intersection (& operator) finds which risky ports are open.
5. Conditional reporting flags dangerous services.

On your Kali VM, try the real thing: nmap -sT 127.0.0.1 — it does exactly this but with actual network connections.
Nmap checks thousands of ports using the same loop + condition pattern!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add port categories: 'Well-known (0-1023)', 'Registered (1024-49151)', 'Dynamic (49152-65535)'. Classify each open port.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Log File Analyser
complete_examples.description: Parse and analyse security log entries using loops and conditions
complete_examples.code:
# Security Log Analyser — parse, filter, and report

# Simulated log entries
logs = [
    "2025-02-28 08:15:22 LOGIN_SUCCESS user=admin ip=10.0.0.5",
    "2025-02-28 08:15:45 LOGIN_FAILED user=admin ip=203.0.113.42",
    "2025-02-28 08:15:46 LOGIN_FAILED user=admin ip=203.0.113.42",
    "2025-02-28 08:15:47 LOGIN_FAILED user=admin ip=203.0.113.42",
    "2025-02-28 09:00:00 FILE_ACCESS user=john ip=10.0.0.12 file=secret.pdf",
    "2025-02-28 09:30:15 LOGIN_SUCCESS user=john ip=10.0.0.12",

    "2025-02-28 10:00:01 LOGIN_FAILED user=root ip=198.51.100.77",
    "2025-02-28 11:45:00 LOGOUT user=admin ip=10.0.0.5",
    "2025-02-28 12:00:00 LOGIN_FAILED user=admin ip=203.0.113.42",
]

# Analysis counters
total = len(logs)
failed = 0
success = 0
suspicious_ips = {}

print("=== SECURITY LOG ANALYSIS ===")
print(f"Total entries: {total}\n")

# Process each log entry
for line in logs:
    if "LOGIN_FAILED" in line:
        failed += 1
        # Extract IP address
        parts = line.split("ip=")
        if len(parts) > 1:
            ip = parts[1].split()[0]
            suspicious_ips[ip] = suspicious_ips.get(ip, 0) + 1
    elif "LOGIN_SUCCESS" in line:
        success += 1

# Report
print(f"Login Success: {success}")
print(f"Login Failed:  {failed}")
print(f"Fail Rate:     {failed/(failed+success)*100:.1f}%")

print(f"\n--- Suspicious IPs (multiple failures) ---")
for ip, count in sorted(suspicious_ips.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True):
    threat = "HIGH" if count >= 3 else "MEDIUM" if count >= 2 else "LOW"
    print(f"  {ip:<20} Failures: {count:<4} Threat: {threat}")
    if count >= 3:
        print(f"  >>> RECOMMEND: Block this IP immediately!")

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Password Strength Validator
complete_examples.description: Check passwords against multiple rules using conditions
complete_examples.code:
# Password Strength Validator
# Checks multiple security rules

def check_password(password):
    """Validate password against security rules."""
    results = []
    score = 0
    
    # Rule 1: Minimum length
    if len(password) >= 8:
        results.append(("Length >= 8", True))
        score += 1
    else:
        results.append(("Length >= 8", False))
    
    # Rule 2: Has uppercase
    has_upper = False
    for char in password:
        if char.isupper():
            has_upper = True
            break
    results.append(("Has uppercase", has_upper))
    if has_upper: score += 1
    
    # Rule 3: Has lowercase
    has_lower = any(c.islower() for c in password)
    results.append(("Has lowercase", has_lower))
    if has_lower: score += 1
    
    # Rule 4: Has digit
    has_digit = any(c.isdigit() for c in password)
    results.append(("Has digit", has_digit))
    if has_digit: score += 1
    
    # Rule 5: Has special character
    specials = "!@#$%^&*()_+-=[]{}|;:,.<>?"
    has_special = any(c in specials for c in password)
    results.append(("Has special char", has_special))
    if has_special: score += 1
    
    return results, score

# Test passwords
test_passwords = ["password", "Abc123", "MyP@ss123!", "short", "NoSpecial123"]

for pwd in test_passwords:
    results, score = check_password(pwd)
    
    # Determine strength
    if score >= 5:
        strength = "STRONG"
    elif score >= 3:
        strength = "MEDIUM"
    else:
        strength = "WEAK"
    
    print(f"\nPassword: {'*' * len(pwd)} ({len(pwd)} chars)")
    for rule, passed in results:
        icon = "✓" if passed else "✗"
        print(f"  {icon} {rule}")
    print(f"  Score: {score}/5 — {strength}")

Password: ******** (8 chars)
  ✓ Length >= 8
  ✗ Has uppercase
  ✓ Has lowercase
  ✗ Has digit
  ✗ Has special char
  Score: 2/5 — WEAK

Password: ****** (6 chars)
  ✗ Length >= 8
  ✓ Has uppercase
  ✓ Has lowercase
  ✓ Has digit
  ✗ Has special char
  Score: 3/5 — MEDIUM
...
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This combines conditions, loops, and strings beautifully:
1. Each rule is an if/else that checks ONE requirement.
2. The for loop with break finds the first uppercase character efficiently.
3. any() with a generator is a Pythonic shortcut: any(c.isdigit() for c in password) = 'is there at least one digit?'
4. Results are stored as tuples (rule_name, passed_boolean) for clean reporting.
5. Score-based classification uses if/elif/else.

This is how real password validators work! Tools like John the Ripper and Hashcat test passwords against similar rules. The difference is they CRACK passwords — you're VALIDATING them.

This demonstrates the LOGIC of brute-force attacks:
1. range(10000) generates all PINs from 0 to 9999.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: while Loop — Network Monitor
complete_examples.description: Continuous monitoring pattern using while loop with exit conditions
complete_examples.code:
# Network Connection Monitor — while loop pattern
import random
import time

print("NETWORK MONITOR v1.0")
print("=" * 50)
print("Monitoring connections... (max 8 checks)\n")

# Monitoring state
check_count = 0
max_checks = 8
alerts = 0
blocked = []

while check_count < max_checks:
    check_count += 1
    
    # Simulate incoming connections
    ips = ["10.0.0.5", "203.0.113.42", "192.168.1.100", "198.51.100.1"]
    ports = [22, 80, 443, 3306, 4444, 8080]
    
    ip = random.choice(ips)
    port = random.choice(ports)
    
    # Decision logic
    is_internal = ip.startswith("10.") or ip.startswith("192.168.")
    is_suspicious_port = port in [4444, 3306, 22]
    
    if not is_internal and is_suspicious_port:
        status = "BLOCKED"
        alerts += 1
        blocked.append(f"{ip}:{port}")
    elif not is_internal:
        status = "WATCHED"
    else:
        status = "ALLOWED"
    
    icon = {"BLOCKED": "🔴", "WATCHED": "🟡", "ALLOWED": "🟢"}[status]
    internal = "INT" if is_internal else "EXT"
    print(f"  [{check_count:>2}] {icon} {ip:<16}:{port:<5} [{internal}] {status}")

print(f"\n{'=' * 50}")
print(f"Checks: {check_count} | Alerts: {alerts} | Blocked: {len(blocked)}")
if blocked:
    print(f"Blocked connections:")
    for b in blocked:
        print(f"  🔴 {b}")
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This shows the while loop monitoring pattern:
1. while check_count < max_checks: runs until the limit is reached.
2. Each iteration simulates an incoming connection.
3. Conditions classify each connection: internal vs external, safe vs suspicious port.
4. Combined conditions (not is_internal AND is_suspicious_port) create complex rules.
5. Counters and lists track alerts and blocked connections.

Real IDS/IPS systems (Snort, Suricata) run essentially this pattern — an infinite while loop that inspects every network packet against a set of rules. The max_checks limit here prevents an infinite loop for the demo.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Nested Loops — Caesar Cipher Cracker
complete_examples.description: Try all 25 possible shifts to crack a Caesar cipher
complete_examples.code:
# Caesar Cipher Cracker — try all 25 shifts
# Caesar cipher shifts each letter by N positions

def caesar_decrypt(ciphertext, shift):
    """Decrypt a Caesar cipher with given shift."""
    result = ""
    for char in ciphertext:
        if char.isalpha():
            base = ord('A') if char.isupper() else ord('a')
            decrypted = chr((ord(char) - base - shift) % 26 + base)
            result += decrypted
        else:
            result += char  # Keep non-letters as-is
    return result

# The encrypted message
ciphertext = "Wkh vhfuhw sdvvzrug lv iruhqvlfv"

print("CAESAR CIPHER CRACKER")
print("=" * 50)
print(f"Ciphertext: {ciphertext}")
print(f"Trying all 25 shifts...\n")

# Common English words to detect correct decryption
common_words = ["the", "is", "and", "was", "for", "that", "password", "secret"]

found = False
for shift in range(1, 26):  # Try shifts 1-25
    decrypted = caesar_decrypt(ciphertext, shift)
    
    # Check if decrypted text contains common words
    lower = decrypted.lower()
    matches = sum(1 for word in common_words if word in lower)
    
    indicator = "" 
    if matches >= 2:
        indicator = " <<< LIKELY MATCH!"
        found = True
    
    print(f"  Shift {shift:>2}: {decrypted}{indicator}")
    
    if found:
        print(f"\nDECRYPTED (shift {shift}): {decrypted}")
        break

if not found:
    print("\nNo obvious match found — may need frequency analysis.")

CAESAR CIPHER CRACKER
==================================================
Ciphertext: Wkh vhfuhw sdvvzrug lv iruhqvlfv
Trying all 25 shifts...

  Shift  1: Vjg ugetgv rcuuyqtf ku hqtgpukeu
  Shift  2: Uif tfdsef qbttvpse jt gpsfotjdt
  Shift  3: The secret password is forensics <<< LIKELY MATCH!

DECRYPTED (shift 3): The secret password is forensics
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This combines nested iterations beautifully:
1. OUTER loop (for shift in range(1, 26)) tries each possible shift value.
2. INNER loop (for char in ciphertext inside the function) processes each character.
3. The ASCII maths: (ord(char) - base - shift) % 26 + base handles the wraparound (Z→A).
4. Detection: checks if decrypted text contains common English words.
5. break exits as soon as a likely match is found — efficiency!

Caesar cipher is the simplest classical cipher. With only 25 possible shifts, brute force is instant. Modern encryption (AES-256) has 2^256 possible keys — that's more than atoms in the universe! On Kali, try: echo 'Wkh vhfuhw' | tr 'A-Za-z' 'X-ZA-Wx-za-w'

List comprehensions are one-line for loops:
[expression for item in list] = transform every item
[expression for item in list if condition] = filter + transform

They're equivalent to:
result = []
for item in list:
    if condition:
        result.append(expression)

But MUCH more compact and Pythonic! Professional Python code uses comprehensions extensively.

complete_examples.title: Example 11: for/else — Search Until Found
complete_examples.description: Python's unique for/else pattern for search-and-report operations
complete_examples.code:
# for/else — Python's unique search pattern
# The else block runs if the loop completed WITHOUT break

# Search for a specific hash in a database
hash_database = [
    {"hash": "abc123", "malware": "WannaCry", "severity": "CRITICAL"},
    {"hash": "def456", "malware": "Stuxnet", "severity": "CRITICAL"},
    {"hash": "ghi789", "malware": "Emotet", "severity": "HIGH"},
    {"hash": "jkl012", "malware": "Mirai", "severity": "HIGH"},
]

# Search function
def lookup_hash(target_hash):
    print(f"\nSearching for: {target_hash}")
    for entry in hash_database:
        if entry["hash"] == target_hash:
            print(f"  MATCH FOUND!")
            print(f"  Malware: {entry['malware']}")
            print(f"  Severity: {entry['severity']}")
            break
    else:
        # This runs ONLY if break was NOT called
        print(f"  No match — file appears clean")

# Test with known malware hash
lookup_hash("def456")

# Test with unknown hash
lookup_hash("zzz999")

# Test with another match
lookup_hash("ghi789")
complete_examples.output:
Searching for: def456
  MATCH FOUND!
  Malware: Stuxnet
  Severity: CRITICAL

Searching for: zzz999
  No match — file appears clean

Searching for: ghi789
  MATCH FOUND!
  Malware: Emotet
  Severity: HIGH
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Python's for/else is unique and powerful:
- The else block after a for loop runs ONLY if the loop completed normally (no break).
- If break IS called, the else is skipped.

This is PERFECT for search patterns:
- Loop through database, if found → break (else skipped).
- If not found → loop ends normally → else runs.

Without for/else, you'd need a 'found' flag variable:
found = False
for entry in db:
    if match: found = True; break
if not found: print('not found')

for/else is cleaner! This pattern is used in hash lookup tools, virus signature databases, and blocklist checking.

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Complete Intrusion Detection Rule Engine
complete_examples.description: Putting it ALL together — a mini IDS using every concept from this topic
complete_examples.code:
# === MINI INTRUSION DETECTION SYSTEM ===
# Uses: if/elif/else, and/or/not, for, while, break, continue,
# enumerate, range, in, nested loops, ternary, comprehensions

import random

# Detection rules
RULES = [
    {"name": "Brute Force", "condition": "failed_logins >= 5", "severity": "HIGH"},
    {"name": "Port Scan", "condition": "unique_ports >= 10", "severity": "HIGH"},
    {"name": "Data Exfil", "condition": "bytes_out > 10000000", "severity": "CRITICAL"},
    {"name": "Suspicious Port", "condition": "port in [4444, 5555, 6666]", "severity": "MEDIUM"},
]

# Simulated network events
events = [
    {"ip": "203.0.113.42", "port": 22, "action": "LOGIN_FAILED", "bytes": 0},
    {"ip": "203.0.113.42", "port": 22, "action": "LOGIN_FAILED", "bytes": 0},
    {"ip": "203.0.113.42", "port": 22, "action": "LOGIN_FAILED", "bytes": 0},
    {"ip": "203.0.113.42", "port": 22, "action": "LOGIN_FAILED", "bytes": 0},
    {"ip": "203.0.113.42", "port": 22, "action": "LOGIN_FAILED", "bytes": 0},
    {"ip": "10.0.0.5", "port": 443, "action": "UPLOAD", "bytes": 15000000},
    {"ip": "198.51.100.1", "port": 4444, "action": "CONNECT", "bytes": 1024},
    {"ip": "10.0.0.12", "port": 80, "action": "LOGIN_SUCCESS", "bytes": 500},
]

print("╔" + "═" * 56 + "╗")
print(f"║{'SYNAPSE INTRUSION DETECTION SYSTEM':^56}║")
print("╠" + "═" * 56 + "╣")

# Phase 1: Process events
print(f"║{'Phase 1: Processing Events':^56}║")
print("╠" + "═" * 56 + "╣")

ip_stats = {}
for i, event in enumerate(events, 1):
    ip = event["ip"]
    if ip not in ip_stats:
        ip_stats[ip] = {"failed": 0, "ports": set(), "bytes_out": 0, "events": 0}
    
    ip_stats[ip]["events"] += 1
    ip_stats[ip]["ports"].add(event["port"])
    
    if event["action"] == "LOGIN_FAILED":
        ip_stats[ip]["failed"] += 1
    if event["action"] == "UPLOAD":
        ip_stats[ip]["bytes_out"] += event["bytes"]

# Phase 2: Check rules
print(f"║{'Phase 2: Checking Rules':^56}║")
print("╠" + "═" * 56 + "╣")

alerts = []
for ip, stats in ip_stats.items():
    is_internal = ip.startswith("10.") or ip.startswith("192.168.")
    
    # Check each rule
    if stats["failed"] >= 5:
        alerts.append((ip, "Brute Force", "HIGH", f"{stats['failed']} failed logins"))
    
    if stats["bytes_out"] > 10_000_000 and is_internal:
        mb = stats["bytes_out"] / 1_000_000
        alerts.append((ip, "Data Exfiltration", "CRITICAL", f"{mb:.1f} MB uploaded"))
    
    for port in stats["ports"]:
        if port in [4444, 5555, 6666] and not is_internal:
            alerts.append((ip, "Suspicious Port", "MEDIUM", f"Port {port}"))

# Phase 3: Report
print(f"║{'Phase 3: Alert Report':^56}║")
print("╠" + "═" * 56 + "╣")

if alerts:
    # Sort by severity
    severity_order = {"CRITICAL": 0, "HIGH": 1, "MEDIUM": 2, "LOW": 3}
    alerts.sort(key=lambda a: severity_order.get(a[2], 4))
    
    for ip, rule, sev, detail in alerts:
        icon = {"CRITICAL": "🔴", "HIGH": "🟠", "MEDIUM": "🟡"}.get(sev, "⚪")
        print(f"║ {icon} [{sev:<8}] {rule:<20} {ip:<15} ║")
        print(f"║   Detail: {detail:<44}║")
else:
    print(f"║{'No threats detected':^56}║")

print("╠" + "═" * 56 + "╣")
print(f"║  Events processed: {len(events):<35}║")
print(f"║  Unique IPs: {len(ip_stats):<41}║")
print(f"║  Alerts generated: {len(alerts):<35}║")
print("╚" + "═" * 56 + "╝")
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This is a COMPLETE mini IDS combining EVERYTHING:

Phase 1 (Data Collection):
- for loop with enumerate iterates events
- Dictionary tracks stats per IP
- set() tracks unique ports
- if conditions classify event types

Phase 2 (Rule Engine):
- Nested loops: outer = IPs, inner = ports
- Complex conditions with and/or
- Threshold checks (>=5 failures, >10MB)

Phase 3 (Reporting):
- Sorting with lambda (severity order)
- Formatted output with conditional icons
- Summary statistics

This is the architecture of real IDS tools like Snort and Suricata — event collection, rule matching, and alert generation. You just built one from scratch! 🎉

visual_diagram.title: Conditions & Loops — Decision & Iteration Flow
visual_diagram.content:
🧠 CONDITIONS & LOOPS — DECISION FLOW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
IF / ELIF / ELSE FLOW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Start
│
▼
❓ if condition True?
YES → Run if block ✅ → Skip to end
NO ↓
❓ elif condition True?
YES → Run elif block ✅ → Skip to end
NO ↓
else → Run else block (default)
│
▼
Continue after if/elif/else
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FOR LOOP FLOW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
for item in collection:
│
▼
❓ More items?
YES → Get next item → Run block → Loop back ↑
NO → Exit loop (run else if present)
break = Exit loop immediately (skip else) continue = Skip rest of block, go to next item
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
WHILE LOOP FLOW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
while condition:
│
▼
❓ Condition True?
YES → Run block → Loop back ↑
NO → Exit loop
⚠ DANGER: If condition NEVER becomes False → infinite loop! Always ensure something inside changes the condition.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
COMPARISON & LOGICAL OPERATORS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
== Equal != Not equal < Less than > Greater <= Less or equal >= Greater or equal and Both True or At least one True not Flip True↔False in Is item in collection? not in Is item NOT in collection?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
💡 REMEMBER
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✓ if/elif checks top-to-bottom, stops at FIRST True ✓ for = known iterations, while = unknown iterations ✓ break stops the loop, continue skips current item ✓ range(start, stop) — stop is EXCLUSIVE! ✓ enumerate() adds a counter to any loop ✓ zip() pairs up multiple lists ✓ List comprehension = one-line for loop ✓ == is comparison, = is assignment — don't mix them!

Or use n /= 2 as shorthand.

Solution:

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Password Retry
practice_exercises.description: Allow 3 attempts to enter 'secret'. Print success or lockout.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: While loops keep going! Allow 3 attempts to enter 'secret'. Print success or lockout.
practice_exercises.starter_code:
correct = "secret"
attempts = 0
max_attempts = 3
passwords = ["admin", "password", "secret"]  # Simulated inputs
# TODO: while loop with max 3 attempts
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Attempt 1: admin - WRONG
Attempt 2: password - WRONG
Attempt 3: secret - CORRECT!

hints.message: while attempts < max_attempts: check each password.
Use break when the correct password is found.

Solution:
while attempts < max_attempts:
    guess = passwords[attempts]
    attempts += 1
    if guess == correct:
        print(f"Attempt {attempts}: {guess} - CORRECT!")
        break
    print(f"Attempt {attempts}: {guess} - WRONG")

practice_exercises.ai_intro: While loops keep going! Start with n=27. If even, halve it. If odd, triple and add 1. Stop at 1. Count steps.
practice_exercises.starter_code:

steps = 0
# TODO: Implement Collatz sequence

hints.message: while n != 1: if n%2==0 then n//=2, else n = n*3+1. Count steps.
The Collatz conjecture says this always reaches 1. Nobody has proved it!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: Byte Counter
practice_exercises.description: Count how many times you can divide 1073741824 by 1024 before it reaches 1. (GB→MB→KB→B)
practice_exercises.ai_intro: While loops keep going! Count how many times you can divide 1073741824 by 1024 before it reaches 1. (GB→MB→KB→B)
practice_exercises.starter_code:
value = 1073741824
steps = 0
units = ["bytes", "KB", "MB", "GB"]
# TODO: Divide by 1024 until value reaches 1

hints.message: while value >= 1024: divide by 1024, increment steps.
Print each step with the appropriate unit from the units list.

Solution:
print(f"{value} {units[steps]}")
while value >= 1024:
    value //= 1024

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Threat Scanner
practice_exercises.description: Scan files: flag .exe as HIGH, .zip as MEDIUM, else SAFE. Print summary.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Combining conditions and loops! Scan files: flag .exe as HIGH, .zip as MEDIUM, else SAFE. Print summary.

# TODO: Classify and count each threat level
practice_exercises.expected_output:

b.txt: SAFE
c.zip: MEDIUM
d.exe: HIGH
e.pdf: SAFE
HIGH: 2, MEDIUM: 1, SAFE: 2

hints.message: Loop with if/elif/else inside. Track counts with a dictionary.
if f.endswith('.exe'): HIGH, elif .zip: MEDIUM, else: SAFE

Solution:
counts = {"HIGH": 0, "MEDIUM": 0, "SAFE": 0}
for f in files:
    if f.endswith(".exe"): level = "HIGH"
    elif f.endswith(".zip"): level = "MEDIUM"
    else: level = "SAFE"
    counts[level] += 1
    print(f"{f}: {level}")
print(f"HIGH: {counts['HIGH']}, MEDIUM: {counts['MEDIUM']}, SAFE: {counts['SAFE']}")
